data.title: Hyundai Motor shares fall 2% as November sales plunge 7%
data.description:
Hyundai Motor India shares have gained over 8 percent in the past month.

Shares of Hyundai Motor India slipped over 2 percent to Rs 1,870 in morning trade on December 2 after the company reported a sharp decline in November sales. The drop comes despite it achieving the highest ever monthly rural contribution.

During the month, the Creta-maker registered total monthly sales of 61,252 units, lower by 7 percent from the same month last year when the company told over 65,000 units. The domestic sales of 48,246 units and export sales of 13,006 units also came in lower by 2 and 10 percent, respectively.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

Follow our LIVE blog for all the latest market updates

However, the overall contribution of SUVs to domestic sales remained robust at 68.8 percent in November 2024. Additionally, Hyundai Motor India Limited achieved its highest-ever monthly rural contribution of 22.1 percent, expanding its reach in India’s hinterland.

Last week, two international brokerages Morgan Stanley and JPMorgan initiated coverage on the stock with both dishing out an overweight rating.

JPMorgan highlighted Hyundai Motor as a strong bet on India’s passenger vehicle growth and premiumisation trend. The brokerage expects a recovery in the passenger vehicle sector to pick up in the second half of FY25, which would benefit Hyundai. It also pointed out that Hyundai’s premium product mix, with 68 percent of sales coming from SUVs, boosts its per-unit profitability, giving it an edge over its competitors.

In a similar vein, JPMorgan anticipates market share tailwinds working in Hyundai’s favour in the second half of this fiscal, primarily driven by its new capacity and models. The brokerage also noted that Hyundai’s 20 percent export contribution provides an added cushion against domestic market cyclicality. Additionally, JPMorgan expressed confidence in Hyundai’s capital expenditure efficiency, which it believes will be a key driver for the company’s higher Return on Capital Employed (RoCE).

At about 10 am, shares of the company were trading at Rs 1,899, lower by 1 percent. Hyundai Motor India shares have gained over 8 percent in the past month.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

data.title: RBL Bank shares sink as partnership on co-branded credit cards with Bajaj Finance ends
data.description:
International brokerage Investec maintained its hold call on the bank, but cut its target price to Rs 170 per share.

  • Watchlist
  • Portfolio
  • Message
  • Set Alert
 
 
live
  • bselive
  • nselive
Volume
Todays L/H
More

Private lender RBL Bank shares tumbled four percent in the morning session as the lender along with NBFC Bajaj Finance decided to end their eight-year long partnership for co-branded credit cards.

“RBL Bank Ltd and Bajaj Finance Ltd have been in discussion over the last month, and it was felt that synergies through this co-brand partnership have undergone significant change over time,” the bank said in a release. The bank, however, added that the move won’t change the plans for its credit cards business.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

The lender said the move to stop the partnership is in line with its vision to build a diverse range of collaborations with NBFCs such as Mahindra & Mahindra Finance Limited, TVS Finance Limited as well as consumer brands like IOC and IRCTC.

“The bank remains focused on growing its credit card business as a prominent customer acquisition engine.”

At 9.40 am, RBL Bank shares were trading at Rs 148.65 per share, down 4.05 percent on the NSE.

Follow our live blog to catch all the updates

International brokerage Investec maintained its hold call on the bank, but cut its target price to Rs 170 per share. The broking house remains cautious on RBL Bank as a result of its large exposure to the MFI and CC verticals.

The brokerage noted that Bajaj Finance was its largest sourcing partner. The move was likely prompted by increasingly stringent regulatory requirements for co-branding partners.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

As a result of the move, the bank's headline credit growth could moderate 200 basis points to 13-14 percent in FY25, added Investec. There could be a corresponding decline in NIIs, leading to an estimates downgrade.

Growth is likely to revive in H2FY25, as the bank scales up arrangements with new co-branding partners.

data.title: Dixon Tech share hits 52-week high as co to manufacture Google Pixel
data.description:
Dixon Technologies (India) Ltd

  • Watchlist
  • Portfolio
  • Message
  • Set Alert
 
 
live
  • bselive
  • nselive
Volume
Todays L/H
More

Shares of Dixon Technologies (India) rose nearly 6 pecent to a 52-week high of Rs 16,739 on November 29 after its wholly-owned subsidiary, Padget Electronics, announced plans to commence mass production of Google Pixel smartphones in collaboration with Compal Smart Device India for Compal's client, Google Information Services India. The production is part of a contract manufacturing agreement signed in February with Compal Smart Device India and will take place at Padget's Noida plant.

At 9.30 AM, Dixon Tech's shares were up over 4 percent at Rs 16,482. Year-to-date, Dixon Tech's stock has delivered a stellar 150 percent return, far outpacing the Nifty 50's 11 percent gain.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

Nomura maintained its 'Buy' rating on Dixon Tech with a target price of Rs 18,654, implying an upside potential of 18 percent from the stock's previous closing price of Rs 15,807.

Follow our live blog for all the market action

According to Nomura, "Dixon will have a significant share of business (SOB) of the Pixel in India, especially the relatively affordable A series, which contributed around 65 percent to Pixel's India sales in 2023. In our view, while volumes are low, realisations will be significantly better at Rs 25,000 - Rs 26,000 per unit for Dixon, compared to current average realisations of Rs 9,000 per unit."

Nomura estimates that Pixel production could add Rs 15 billion to Dixon Tech's revenues by FY26, assuming a 60 percent share of business and gradual ramp-up.

Additionally, the brokerage firm believes the move positions Dixon Tech as a strong contender for premium smartphone production, particularly as manufacturers shift away from China due to geopolitical tensions.

Also Read | Dixon Tech and Nokia tap into India's hunger for data, plan to make fixed broadband devices

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

"Given the US-China trade war and the success of Apple in India, if Pixel shifts approximately 30 percent of its business to India, it will imply Rs 90-Rs 100 billion of potential sourcing from India and will be a sizeable opportunity for Dixon as well," Nomura said.

Meanwhile, India's Ministry of Electronics and Technology is considering a Rs 400 billion incentive package to boost local electronic component manufacturing. If approved, this could increase local value addition, especially for components like Printed Circuit Boards (PCBs), camera modules, display sub-assembly, and lithium-ion cells.

Dixon Techn reported a 264 percent year-on-year (YoY) surge in net profit to Rs 411.7 crore for the quarter ended September, including an exceptional gain of Rs 209.6 crore. The company's revenue from operations jumped 133 percent to Rs 11,534 crore.

data.title: Sensex recovers 400 pts from day's low, Nifty pares losses led by auto, realty stocks
data.description:
Maruti Suzuki, Apollo Hospitals, Shriram Finance, Bajaj Auto, and M&M were the top gainers on the Nifty.


Following a weak start, benchmark indices Nifty and Sensex pared almost all losses to trade marginally in the negative after a sharp rally in auto, realty and pharma stocks helped regain momentum. However, bank and IT stocks edged lower as markets entered the last month before the start of the new year. The drop comes despite a sharp rise in US markets.

The S&P 500 rallied 5.14 percent in November -- its best performance since the same month last year. The Nasdaq gained almost a percent on Friday, securing a 6.2 percent monthly rise, the highest since May.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

At about 9:40 am,  the Sensex was down 79,643, down 0.2 percent while the Nifty was down at 24,116, lower by 0.1 percent. On the BSE, 1,666 shares advanced, 1,503 stocks declined and about 163 were unchanged.

Follow our LIVE blog for all the latest market updates

"Despite robust cues, the Indian markets couldn't mirror the trend. However, we've seen a decent price-wise correction and the Nifty will likely trade in a broad range till the end of December," Ruchit Jain, Vice President of Technical Research at Motilal Oswal, said. He added that if markets surpass the immediate hurdle of 25,400-500, it could likely claim 25,000 in the coming weeks.

On Friday, the Sensex rallied 900 points and the Nifty crossed the 24,150 mark. Traders suggest that a host of negative factors—mainly the FII selling—is yet to be contained, and investors should wait and watch for a decisive trend before entering the market. FIIs sold over Rs 4,500 crore, taking their total selloff for November to nearly Rs 46,000 crore.

Also read: Experts trim FY25 GDP target amid slowing consumption, lower capex

Auto stocks were buzzing in trade following the release of November sales data. Newly-listed Hyundai Motor shares traded 2 percent lower after its total sales declined 6.9 percent to 61,252 units compared to 65,801 units. Domestic sales fell 2.4 percent to 48,246, while exports dropped 20.5 percent to 13,006 units.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

India's leading auto player, Maruti Suzuki was trading higher by over 2 percent to top the Nifty index after its sales rose 10.4 percent to 1.81 lakh units from 1.64 lakh units. Domestic sales increased 8.06 percent to 1.52 lakh units. Passenger vehicle sales rose 5.3 percent at 1.41 lakh units against 1.34 lakh units. Exports surged 24.8 percent to 28,633 units.

Read more: Cipla stake sale: 1.72% equity changes hands in block deals; promoters likely sellers

Cochin Shipyard shares rose over 4 percent after the company signed a contract worth Rs 1,000 crore with the Ministry of Defence (MoD), Government of India. The contract is for a short refit and dry docking of a large Indian Naval Vessel. The estimated duration for the project is around 5 months.

Nifty Pharma started the day strong, rising over a percent. Gains in big names such as Sun Pharma, Lupin, Dr Reddy's, and Torrent Pharma lifted the index. Nifty Auto also rallied over a percent largely helped by Maruti Suzuki. A host of auto stocks will be in focus today as they release monthly sales data. Nifty Realty also rose, breaking its two-day losing streak as gains in DLF, Lodha, and Godrej Properties boosted sentiment in the space.

Losers included Nifty Bank, IT, and FMCG, all falling o.4 percent each.

The broader market opened on a strong note, with mid- and small-cap indices rising 0.3 percent and 0.6 percent, respectively. Year-to-date, both indices have gained 21 percent, significantly outperforming the NSE Nifty’s 10.5 percent increase. Analysts believe that after a healthy correction, mid-and small-cap stocks may see renewed buying interest and could continue their upward momentum in the coming weeks.

"We expect the market to remain range-bound amidst mixed global cues and lack of domestic triggers," Siddhartha Khemka of Motilal Oswal, said.

From the technical standpoint, Deepak Jasani, Head of Retail Research at HDFC Securities, suggests that the trend of the Nifty has turned bullish. Immediate resistance is placed around 24,350 levels where multiple tops are placed. In case of correction, Nifty could find support near 23,900 levels.

Maruti Suzuki, Shriram Finance, Apollo Hospitals, Coal India, Dr Reddy's Labs were among major gainers on the Nifty, while losers were Britannia, HUL, ONGC, L&T, TCS.

data.title: India’s GDP misses expectations, but experts see limited market fallout
data.description:
The slowdown in GDP growth was led by weaker industrial performance like mining and manufacturing.

Markets have been factoring the economic slowdown for quite some time and the latest lower-than-expected GDP numbers although surprising are unlikely to have a bearing on the markets. Most market experts Moneycontrol spoke to see limited impact on the markets. The numbers were announced post market hours on November 29.

Despite the disappointing numbers -- 5.4 percent -- Vikas Gupta, CEO and Chief Investment Strategist at OmniScience Capital, remains optimistic on the back of private consumption, which grew at an impressive 6 percent, significantly outpacing overall GDP growth and surpassing the 2.6% recorded in Q2 FY24. “This dispels recent concerns about weakness in private consumption,” Gupta said.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

Also Read | What do the GDP data say about long-term structural changes in the economy?

While government consumption improved from the previous quarter, it remained lower than last year, which Gupta attributed to cautious spending ahead of elections. Despite the softer growth, Gupta believes the dip is temporary, driven by seasonal monsoon effects and election-related factors. He also expressed confidence that the data would not significantly affect equity markets, suggesting that any short-term market dips could create buying opportunities for long-term investors.

However, Sahil Kapoor of DSP Mutual Fund has a more cautious stance, underscoring that GDP is showing signs of slowing. “GDP is slowing and is likely to slow further,” Kapoor said adding that it would be difficult to predict how the markets would react as market movements are primarily driven by corporate earnings, which have been decelerating.

“Nominal growth is slow. GDP data just confirms. It is difficult to predict how it will impact the market as there are many other drivers,” Kapoor explained.

Trivesh D, COO, Tradejini believes that the GDP numbers come at an important juncture, as the economy grapples with slower growth and critical reforms on the horizon. With GDP growth moderating to 5.4% in Q2 FY25, down from 6.7% in Q1, we are seeing the sharpest slowdown in seven quarters, he says, while adding that this deceleration, led by weaker industrial performance like mining and manufacturing raises questions about near-term resilience.

But he also noted that India’s GDP growth of around 6.5% for FY25 still stands strong globally. “The challenge now is sustaining this momentum. I think a sharper focus on boosting consumption, job creation, and widening economic participation is critical to achieving higher potential growth.”

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

Looking ahead, Trivesh feels that the second half of FY25 could see some recovery, particularly with government capex potentially ramping up after a subdued first half. On a broader note, Trivesh also pointed to the upcoming Banking Laws Amendment Bill 2024, which the Finance Minister is set to introduce in the Lok Sabha. He sees this as a significant development. “For the markets, I think this combination of GDP data and significant banking reforms makes for an interesting dynamic,” Trivesh said.

While slower economic growth might weigh on near-term market sentiment, he believes that the anticipation around structural changes in the banking sector could provide a positive offset. “Banking and financial stocks are likely to see increased activity, as investors assess the potential impact of these reforms on sectoral growth and operational efficiency,” he added.

data.title: Economy showing signs of recovery; indicators should improve meaningfully by Feb or March, says Neelkanth Mishra
data.description:
Timely intervention, he believes will be critical. “Once you lose momentum, economic momentum feeds on itself. If we’re too late in reviving growth, potential investments may be shelved, and we might settle at a lower growth rate,” he said.

The India long-term growth story remains promising and the economy is already showing signs of recovery on the back of government spending and easing monetary conditions, said Neelkanth Mishra, Chief Economist, Axis Bank on The Open Dialogue Podcast by Axis Bank.

“Government spending is now hitting the economy, and the RBI’s end of quantitative tightening is showing effects. By February or March, we should see high-frequency indicators improving meaningfully,” Mishra said.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

He further added that the long-term growth outlook remains promising as long as we continue to invest in our infrastructure and growth. “With our young population, advancements in AI, and focus on infrastructure, I don’t think 7 percent sustained growth over the next five years is a challenge if we address these issues decisively,” he said.

Also read: Q2 GDP growth shocker may trigger action from RBI; raise questions over India's potential growth

What is driving the current slowdown?

The slowdown comes from both global and domestic factors, with internal issues taking center stage, he said. “There is a global export problem, but I think the deeper issues are all internal. When you see retail credit, MSME credit, microfinance struggling, you know that this is a fiscal and monetary problem, that there is some contraction that has happened, which I think was mostly unintended,” he said.

This downturn, he said, began earlier this year. “We started slowing around March, April. We are now in the sixth or seventh month. My sense is that we have kind of hit the bottom, meaning things are not worsening anymore.”

Effect of government spending

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

Fiscal consolidation and administrative delays have also played a critical role in deepening the slowdown. “If you increase your fiscal deficit, it boosts growth. If you reduce your fiscal deficit, it takes away from growth. India is still consolidating fiscally,” Mishra explained.

Elections added to the delays. “After late February, when the Model Code of Conduct came in, the cabinet meeting stopped. The file stopped moving. And then for the next three months, there was no government because the elections were going on.”

However, there are signs of recovery. “October spending has picked up meaningfully. And the finance ministry officials are making explicit comments that we will meet our spending targets this year.”

Timely intervention, he believes will be critical. “Once you lose momentum, economic momentum feeds on itself. If we’re too late in reviving growth, potential investments may be shelved, and we might settle at a lower growth rate,” he said.

How a trade war impacts the economy

Global uncertainties, including rising US bond yields and potential trade wars, have compounded India’s challenges. “In the last two months, 10-year U.S. bond yields have risen from 3.6 to 4.4 percent, which is substantial. This has resulted in incessant outflows of funds from India,” Mishra said.

The potential for a US-China trade war could disrupt global supply chains. “Suppose you put a 60% import duty on all Chinese imports. Suddenly, the flow of trade shifts, some ports see more volume, others less. Freight rates rise, and it is like sand in the wheels of global trade. That causes massive disruption.”

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by investment experts on Moneycontrol.com are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol.com advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.

data.title: The Eastern Window: Global markets bear the burnt of overcapacity in Chinese manufacturing
data.description:
The U.S. and E.U. are working on new rules to restrict the flow of Chinese goods.

Highlights:

  • The U.S. and E.U. are working on new rules to restrict the flow of Chinese goods

  • The move may result in heavy dumping of cheap Chinese goods in developing countries like India.

  • Overcapacity is caused by reckless industrial expansion and low domestic demand in China.

  • Much depends on whether Trump would implement his threats about high tariff on Chinese goods.

data.title: What were the drivers of growth in Q2?
data.description:
The biggest contributor to GVA growth in Q2 in the services sector was 'Financial services, real estate and professional services’'.

The services sector accounted for almost three-quarters of the year-on-year growth in Gross Value Added in Q2 of FY25, making it by far the biggest driver of growth in the economy in Q2. Simply put, out of the GVA growth of 5.6 percent in Q2, the contribution of services was 4.1 percentage points. The rest of the economy contributed the balance 1.5 percentage points. That’s how unbalanced growth was in Q2. And if you count construction as

data.title: UBS upgrades Petronet LNG to 'buy' on positive growth outlook, sees 20% upside
data.description:
Petronet LNG reported a 4.17 percent YoY increase in consolidated net profit at Rs 849 crore for Q2.

  • Watchlist
  • Portfolio
  • Message
  • Set Alert
 
 
live
  • bselive
  • nselive
Volume
Todays L/H
More

Gas distributer Petronet LNG shares will be in focus on December 2, after international brokerage UBS upgraded its rating on the Oil & Gas player.

The brokerage upgraded its rating on the Petronet LNG to 'buy' from 'neutral', while upping its target price to Rs 400 per share, from Rs 320 earlier. This implies a potential 20 percent upside from the previous close.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

According to the brokerage, the firm is a beneficiary of India's increasing LNG dependance. Further, it is focusing on expanding capacity at a time when the country needs more LNG. India has secured 5 long-term LNG supply contracts, starting 2026.

The firm's Dahej terminal could handle 19-20 mmtpa LNG, despite intensifying competition.

Follow our market blog to catch all the updates

For the quarter ended September, Petronet LNG reported a 4.17 percent year-on-year increase in consolidated net profit at Rs 849 crore. The gas distributer reported a consolidated net profit of Rs 815 crore in the same period a year ago. Sequentially, net profit was down 23 percent.

Revenue from operations came in at Rs 13,024 crore, a marginal increase from Rs 12,532 crore in the year-ago period.

The overall LNG volume processed by the company in the current quarter was 239 TBTU, as against the LNG volume processed in the corresponding and previous quarters, which stood at 223 TBTU and 262 TBTU, respectively.

Story continues below Advertisement
Remove Ad

During the half year ended September 30, Dahej terminal processed highest-ever 473 TBTU of LNG as against 428 TBTU during the corresponding period last year. The company processed 501 TBTU LNG in the first half of the current financial year, as against 453 TBTU last year.

Over the past year, shares of Petronet LNG have jumped 64 percent as against 16.7 percent rise in the Nifty 50 during the same time period.
